EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with large hail spread northward across East Tennessee during the early morning hours of March 27, reaching southwest Virginia by late morning. These storms were associated with a warm front, which later stalled across northeast Tennessee. This stalled front was the focus of additional thunderstorms that produced damaging winds and  flooding in the evening of March 27 and into March 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of roadways caused vehicles to become stranded. Road closures. Highway 421 near Pennington Gap was impassable. Reports of structures being flooded in Pennington Gap. A 24 hour rainfall measurement of 4.37 inches at Jonesville at 8:00 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved slowly across southwest Virginia ahead of a cold front that swept in from the Ohio Valley later in the morning. Early morning heavy rainfall over eastern Kentucky clipped Wise County Virginia as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water across several roads near the towns of Big Stone Gap and Pound.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure centered from the Midwest into New England helped to supply low level cold air into the area, as a prolonged Classic Cold Air Damming regime was in place throughout the duration of the event. With warmer air present aloft, precipitation fell in the form of freezing rain and sleet across Eastern and Southeast Virginia, as a couple of weak low pressure areas tracked northeast along and off the Southeast and Mid Atlantic Coasts. There were two distinct waves of precipitation that moved across the area. One that occurred during the early morning-midday on the 18th, and a second wave of light to moderate precipitation that moved across the region during the early to mid morning on the 19th. This resulted in ice accretions between a trace and 0.20 inch, along with sleet accumulations between 0.5 inch and 1.0 inch. A few trees and power lines were downed, with some scattered power outages reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accretions between a trace and 0.20 inch were reported. A few trees and power lines were downed, with some scattered power outages reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree was downed on Little Back River Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed near Harborview and downtown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure centered from the Midwest into New England helped to supply low level cold air into the area, as a prolonged Classic Cold Air Damming regime was in place throughout the duration of the event. With warmer air present aloft, precipitation fell in the form of freezing rain and sleet across central and south central Virginia, and the Virginia Northern Neck, as a couple of weak low pressure areas tracked northeast along and off the Southeast and Mid Atlantic Coasts. There were two distinct waves of precipitation that moved across the area. One that occurred during the early morning-midday on the 18th, and a second wave of light to moderate precipitation that moved across the region during the early to mid morning on the 19th. This resulted in significant ice accretion between 0.20 inch and 0.40 inch, along with sleet accumulations between 0.5 inch and 1.5 inches. Several trees and power lines were downed, with numerous power outages reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accretions between 0.20 inch and 0.30 inch were reported. Several trees and power lines were downed, with numerous power outages reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface high pressure centered from the Midwest into New England helped to supply low level cold air into the area, as a prolonged Classic Cold Air Damming regime was in place throughout the duration of the event. With warmer air present aloft, precipitation fell in the form of freezing rain and sleet across central and south central Virginia, and the Virginia Northern Neck, as a couple of weak low pressure areas tracked northeast along and off the Southeast and Mid Atlantic Coasts. There were two distinct waves of precipitation that moved across the area. One that occurred during the early morning-midday on the 18th, and a second wave of light to moderate precipitation that moved across the region during the early to mid morning on the 19th. This resulted in significant ice accretion between 0.20 inch and 0.40 inch, along with sleet accumulations between 0.5 inch and 1.5 inches. Several trees and power lines were downed, with numerous power outages reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accretions between 0.20 inch and 0.30 inch, along with sleet accumulations between 0.5 inch and 1.0 inch were reported. Several trees and power lines were downed, with numerous power outages reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system brought with it deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico as it moved across the central Appalachians, overriding a shallow layer of sub-freezing temperatures within high pressure established across the lower mid-Atlantic. This resulted in widespread freezing rain across the large portions of the Virginia, as well as a mixture of freezing rain, sleet and some snow further west across the mountains. Freezing rain accumulations across portions of Southside Virginia were the highest experienced in several years, ranging from widespread 0.25 to 0.5 inch accretions, with locally higher accretions of 1 inch in thickness.  The icing from this freezing rain weighed down trees and tree limbs, as well as power lines and power poles, causing a great many of them to snap.  For some counties, there were reports that nearly 90 percent of the residents lost power, with some remaining without power for over a week.  Numerous automobile wrecks were noted due to ice causing slick roadway surfaces.  Some injuries were noted, but no known fatalities occurred due to this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations provided by the National Weather Service and by members of the general public indicate ice accumulations from freezing rain of around 0.20 inches in thickness widespread across portions of Montgomery County, including around the communities of Blacksburg, Merrimac and Christiansburg. These were in combination with sleet accumulations of 0.4 to 1 inch depth for the same locations. The icing resulted in over 2,679 power outages within the county.  Numerous trees and tree limbs were also brought down by the weight of the icing.  Key impacts: power outages; hazardous to impossible travel conditions; widespread tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system brought deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico as it moved across the central Appalachians, overriding a shallow layer of sub-freezing temperatures within high pressure established across the lower mid-Atlantic. This resulted in a widespread mixture of freezing rain, sleet and snow across the large portions of western Virginia only days after a similar winter storm moved across the region. Freezing rain accumulations were highest east of the Blue Ridge, with locally higher accretions of 0.5 inch in thickness.  A thicker layer of cold air along and west of the Blue Ridge resulted in less freezing rain and more sleet, as well as mainly snow along and north of Interstate 64 west of the city of Lexington. The icing from this freezing rain weighed down trees and tree limbs, as well as power lines and power poles, causing a great many of them to snap.  The power grid was already fragile from heavy ice accumulations from a few days before, and many communities lost power again due to downed powerlines that had only recently been restored.  Multiple trees and tree limbs were snapped by the weight of ice accumulations. The widespread winter storm conditions resulted in numerous travel delays, with schools across the region closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Skywarn-trained weather spotters and members of the general public observed ice accumulations from freezing rain ranging from 0.25 to 0.3 inches in thickness across portions of Wythe County, including around the communities of Wytheville and Coleman Store. Sleet accumulations of 1 inch were also observed near the community of Rural Retreat.  The icing resulted in at least 1,125 known power outages within the county.  Key impacts: power outages; hazardous travel conditions; tree damage; schools closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple waves of energy passing along a stalled frontal boundary near the Ohio River resulted in several rounds of prolonged and, at times, heavy rain across the northern half of Bath County.  In the 48 hour period ending 10 am on the 28th, the northern half of Bath County had already received between 2 to locally 3.25 inches of rainfall from shower and thunderstorm activity. Snow pack prior to the arrival of the rainfall was confined to the highest ridge tops, where locally up to 4 inches was on the ground.  By the morning of the 28th, soils were almost completely saturated, resulting in efficient runoff of rainfall from showers and thunderstorms that would occur during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The IFLOWS gauge at the Bath County Pump Storage Station observed 0.12 inches of rainfall on the 27th and 2.28 inches rainfall on the 28th for a 48-hour total of 2.40 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure brought rain and wintry precipitation to the region February 15th into the 16th. A few instances of minor flooding were observed, but nothing too widespread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gage on Accotink Creek at Strathmeade Springs, VA. rose above flood stage during the specified time, reaching a max flood stage of 252.10 feet at 1:15 AM EST. At 251.5 feet elevation, Woodburn Road is covered by approximately six inches of water at its lowest point. Portions of the Cross County Trail are also flooded. At an elevation of 252.5 feet, both Woodburn Road and Prosperity Avenue become flooded by at least six inches of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low pressure system was observed stretching southward from the Great Lakes along the Appalachian Chain, supporting deepening surface low pressure off the mid-Atlantic coast. This resulted in sustained northwesterly wind flow across the central Appalachians. Moisture carried from Lake Michigan provided the fuel for accumulating mountain snowfall while a strong disturbance rotating around the upper low provided the energy to support high wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of as much as 6 inches were observed at elevations above 3,500 feet across portions of Grayson County, including at the Mount Rogers National Recreation Area.  Key Impacts: transportation delays from both the winds and snowfall, traffic accidents and delays, localized power outages.

For some counties, there were reports that nearly 90 percent of the residents lost power, with some remaining without power for over a week.  Numerous automobile wrecks were noted due to ice causing slick roadway surfaces.  Some injuries were noted, but no known fatalities occurred due to this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Skywarn-trained weather spotters and members of the general public observed ice accumulations from freezing rain ranging from 0.25 to 0.5 inches in thickness across nearly all of Charlotte County, including in the communities of Keysville, Madisonville, and Charlotte Court House.  Over 5,000 power outages were reported at one point.  It was estimated that as much as 88 percent of the county's residents were without power by the end of the storm, with around 154 power poles snapped and 221 power lines brought down due to the weight of the ice.  Some residents were without power for a week before it was restored.  As a result, each of the county's seven fire stations were set up as warming and charging stations for the local residents. Thousands of trees and tree limbs were also brought down by the icing.  Key impacts: power outages; hazardous to impossible travel conditions; widespread tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light winds and excessive low level moisture allowed areas of dense fog to form during the evening of the 28th. The fog dissipated before dawn on the 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light winds and excessive low level moisture allowed areas of dense fog to form during the evening of the 28th. The fog dissipated before dawn on the 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple waves of energy passing along a stalled frontal boundary near the Ohio River resulted in several rounds of prolonged and, at times, heavy rain across the northern half of Bath County.  In the 48 hour period ending 10 am on the 28th, the northern half of Bath County had already received between 2 to locally 3.25 inches of rainfall from shower and thunderstorm activity. Snow pack prior to the arrival of the rainfall was confined to the highest ridge tops, where locally up to 4 inches was on the ground.  By the morning of the 28th, soils were almost completely saturated, resulting in efficient runoff of rainfall from showers and thunderstorms that would occur during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jordan Top IFLOWS gauge recorded a rainfall amount of 1.56 inches on the 27th and 1.12 inches of the 28th, for a 48-hour rainfall total of 2.68 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low pressure system was observed stretching southward from the Great Lakes along the Appalachian Chain, supporting deepening surface low pressure off the mid-Atlantic coast. This resulted in sustained northwesterly wind flow across the central Appalachians. Moisture carried from Lake Michigan provided the fuel for accumulating mountain snowfall while a strong disturbance rotating around the upper low provided the energy to support high wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple wind gusts of 58 mph or greater were measured by automated weather sensors between mile markers 2 and 3 along Interstate 77 near the community of Fancy Gap, the highest of which was 64 mph.  These wind gusts caused four known tractor trailers to overturn along this stretch of the interstate at the times of 5:38 am, 6:18 am, 7:47 am, and 9:03 am EST.  One of the drivers was injured with non-life threatening injuries, but the other drivers were unharmed during the incidents. At least two trees were also blown down in the nearby area by these winds: one along Paul's Creek Road near the community of Cana and the other along the interstate exit ramp for Piper's Gap Road.  Key Impacts: transportation delays from both the winds and snowfall, traffic accidents and delays, downed trees and localized power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system brought deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico as it moved across the central Appalachians, overriding a shallow layer of sub-freezing temperatures within high pressure established across the lower mid-Atlantic. This resulted in a widespread mixture of freezing rain, sleet and snow across the large portions of western Virginia only days after a similar winter storm moved across the region. Freezing rain accumulations were highest east of the Blue Ridge, with locally higher accretions of 0.5 inch in thickness.  A thicker layer of cold air along and west of the Blue Ridge resulted in less freezing rain and more sleet, as well as mainly snow along and north of Interstate 64 west of the city of Lexington. The icing from this freezing rain weighed down trees and tree limbs, as well as power lines and power poles, causing a great many of them to snap.  The power grid was already fragile from heavy ice accumulations from a few days before, and many communities lost power again due to downed powerlines that had only recently been restored.  Multiple trees and tree limbs were snapped by the weight of ice accumulations. The widespread winter storm conditions resulted in numerous travel delays, with schools across the region closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Skywarn-trained weather spotters and members of the general public observed ice accumulations from freezing rain of 0.25 inches in thickness across portions of Botetourt County, including around the communities of Fincastle, Lithia, and Daleville. Sleet accumulations of 0.8 to 1.5 inches were also observed in these same communities. Key impacts: power outages; hazardous travel conditions; tree damage; schools closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper level low pressure system was observed stretching southward from the Great Lakes along the Appalachian Chain, supporting a deep surface low pressure system off the Atlantic coast.  A strong upper-level disturbance passed around the upper low across the central Appalachians, providing the energy necessary to cause localized high wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated weather sensor located in the Bent Mountain area of Roanoke County measured a single peak wind gust of 60 mph.  Several other gusts were observed in the 45 to 55 mph range. Key Impacts: transportation delays and a few power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough passed quickly across the central Appalachians early on February 7th, supporting weak low pressure west of the mountains that gave way to rapidly deepening low pressure along the Carolina coast. Limited amounts of moisture and cold air kept the heaviest accumulations of snowfall confined to the mountains and foothills, as well as portions of the Piedmont north of Highway 460. Given warm air aloft moving across the region with the storm, precipitation types often varied between snow, sleet and ice from freezing rain, with most observers witnessing more than one precipitation type.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple Skywarn-trained weather spotters, Cooperative Weather Observers, and members of the general public observed snowfall accumulations of 3 to 4 inches across Tazewell County, VA.  This snowfall was intermixed with brief periods of freezing rain, as well as up to one half inch of sleet in spots.  These observations originated from around the communities of Tazewell, Burkes Garden, Bluefield and Gratton.  Key Impacts: Travel delays due to snow, sleet and ice covering the roadway surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough passed quickly across the central Appalachians early on February 7th, supporting weak low pressure west of the mountains that gave way to rapidly deepening low pressure along the Carolina coast. Limited amounts of moisture and cold air kept the heaviest accumulations of snowfall confined to the mountains and foothills, as well as portions of the Piedmont north of Highway 460. Given warm air aloft moving across the region with the storm, precipitation types often varied between snow, sleet and ice from freezing rain, with most observers witnessing more than one precipitation type.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple Skywarn-trained weather spotters, a Cooperative Weather Observer, and a CoCoRaHS Observer observed snowfall accumulations of 3.5 to nearly 5 inches across Rockbridge County, VA.  This snowfall was intermixed with brief periods of freezing rain, as well as up to one half inch of sleet in spots.  These observations originated from around the communities of Vesuvius, the City of Lexington, and Rockbridge Baths.  Key Impacts: Travel delays due to snow, sleet and ice covering the roadway surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system brought deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico as it moved across the central Appalachians, overriding a shallow layer of sub-freezing temperatures within high pressure established across the lower mid-Atlantic. This resulted in a widespread mixture of freezing rain, sleet and snow across the large portions of western Virginia only days after a similar winter storm moved across the region. Freezing rain accumulations were highest east of the Blue Ridge, with locally higher accretions of 0.5 inch in thickness.  A thicker layer of cold air along and west of the Blue Ridge resulted in less freezing rain and more sleet, as well as mainly snow along and north of Interstate 64 west of the city of Lexington. The icing from this freezing rain weighed down trees and tree limbs, as well as power lines and power poles, causing a great many of them to snap.  The power grid was already fragile from heavy ice accumulations from a few days before, and many communities lost power again due to downed powerlines that had only recently been restored.  Multiple trees and tree limbs were snapped by the weight of ice accumulations. The widespread winter storm conditions resulted in numerous travel delays, with schools across the region closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Skywarn-trained weather spotters and members of the general public observed ice accumulations from freezing rain ranging from 0.25 to 0.30 inches in thickness across portions of Carroll County, including around the communities of Pipers Gap and Gladesboro. Key impacts: power outages; hazardous travel conditions; tree damage; schools closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system brought with it deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico as it moved across the central Appalachians, overriding a shallow layer of sub-freezing temperatures within high pressure established across the lower mid-Atlantic. This was in addition to reports of up to 3 inches of snow and sleet which accumulated early during the storm.  Numerous trees and tree limbs were also brought down by the weight of the icing.  Key impacts: power outages; hazardous to impossible travel conditions; widespread tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system moving along a stationary frontal boundary brought about periods of moderate to heavy snow across far southern West Virginia and down the spine of the Appalachians into southwest Virginia late on the 6th and into the early morning hours of the 7th. Snowfall rates of one inch an hour occurred before sunrise, prompting accumulation totals ranging from 4 to 8 inches in southwest Virginia. This was particularly true in Dickenson County, where a burst of snowfall resulted in almost 9 inches to fall between 4 and 9 AM. This resulted in treacherous road conditions with several motorists becoming stranded on primary roads in the county.
A cold front sparked scattered severe thunderstorms that produced mainly damaging straight-line wind gusts across much of Virginia into parts of Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down in the 800 block of VA-671 Spottswood Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance passed across the central Appalachians and lower mid-Atlantic during the late evening hours of the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th, resulting in a mixture of snow, sleet and ice from freezing rain.  Accumulations from this event ranged from around 1 to 2 inches of snow and sleet, with less than a tenth of an inch of icing.  This caused little to no damage, with only isolated/sporadic power outages reported.  Travel was impacted however, with multiple automobile accidents reported in Campbell County.  Injuries resulting from these accidents, if any, were considered to be minor.  The wintry conditions resulted in school closures across region.  COVID-19 vaccination centers in Lynchburg were also closed due to the difficult travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least four traffic accidents were reported by the City of Lynchburg Police Department as a result of slick roadway surfaces caused by a combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain having fallen during the late evening hours of the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th.  No injuries, if any occurred, were considered to be serious.  A few power outages were also reported in the city.  Key Impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, school closures, isolated/sporadic power outages.

The icing resulted in over 27,000 power outages across both the county and in Danville.  Power was out to some homes for as long as 9 days, with repair crews coming from as far away as South Carolina to help restore power, with some workers putting in 130 hours of work over 8-9 days until all power was restored.  One lineman was injured while performing the repairs.  Numerous utility poles were replaced because of icing damage, including 6 poles around the Orphanage Road and Iris Lane area of Danville, and 4 poles along Twin Arch Drive.  Numerous automobile accidents were also reported.  Thousands of trees and tree limbs were also brought down by the weight of the icing across both the city and the county.  Key impacts: power outages; hazardous to impossible travel conditions; automobile accidents; widespread tree damage; injury to repair personnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms trained across southern Highland County, forming in an environment of CAPE exceeding 2,500 J/Kg and Precipitable Water of around 1.5 inches.  The storms produced 2.5 to 4 inches of rainfall amounts over a 2 to 3-hour period, with rainfall rates at times reaching 5 to 6 inches per hour.  Steep terrain in the Back Creek basin aided in creating efficient runoff to cause the stream gauge at Back Creek at Sunrise to rise to 10.01 feet, exceeding Moderate Flood Stage set at 10 feet.  This is the highest this gauge has risen since 2008.  FLASH estimated that rainfall just upstream from the stream gauge was over 3 times greater than 1-hour Flash Flood Guidance.  CREST Unit Streamflow was estimating over 1,000 cfs per square mile, whiile CREST 1-hour Average Return Interval estimates put this rainfall at well over a 200-year event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gauge at Back Creek at Sunrise (SUNV2) crested at the Moderate Flood Stage of 10.01 feet (4,370 cfs) at 8:00 pm EST, the highest level observed at this gauge since 2008.  This crest was less than a 5-year event per USGS StreamStats estimates for this gauge.  No impacts of the flooding were reported, perhaps due to the rural nature of land use surrounding the gauge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms trained across southern Highland County, forming in an environment of CAPE exceeding 2,500 J/Kg and Precipitable Water of around 1.5 inches.  The storms produced 2.5 to 4 inches of rainfall amounts over a 2 to 3-hour period, with rainfall rates at times reaching 5 to 6 inches per hour.  Steep terrain in the Back Creek basin aided in creating efficient runoff to cause the stream gauge at Back Creek at Sunrise to rise to 10.01 feet, exceeding Moderate Flood Stage set at 10 feet.  This is the highest this gauge has risen since 2008.  FLASH estimated that rainfall just upstream from the stream gauge was over 3 times greater than 1-hour Flash Flood Guidance.  CREST Unit Streamflow was estimating over 1,000 cfs per square mile, whiile CREST 1-hour Average Return Interval estimates put this rainfall at well over a 200-year event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station in the Big Island area (Weather Underground: KVABIGIS8) recorded a rainfall amount from a thunderstorm of 2.76 occurring in less than 1 hour.  This amount is between a 50 and 100-year rainfall event per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms trained across southern Highland County, forming in an environment of CAPE exceeding 2,500 J/Kg and Precipitable Water of around 1.5 inches.  The storms produced 2.5 to 4 inches of rainfall amounts over a 2 to 3-hour period, with rainfall rates at times reaching 5 to 6 inches per hour.  Steep terrain in the Back Creek basin aided in creating efficient runoff to cause the stream gauge at Back Creek at Sunrise to rise to 10.01 feet, exceeding Moderate Flood Stage set at 10 feet.  This is the highest this gauge has risen since 2008.  FLASH estimated that rainfall just upstream from the stream gauge was over 3 times greater than 1-hour Flash Flood Guidance.  CREST Unit Streamflow was estimating over 1,000 cfs per square mile, whiile CREST 1-hour Average Return Interval estimates put this rainfall at well over a 200-year event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm caused Indian Run to flood out of its banks and flow across Fontella Road, causing the road to become impassible.  The road was reopened after the flood waters receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the lower Mid-Atlantic during the afternoon and evening of June 10th, forming in an environment of strong afternoon heating in a moist atmosphere.  Storm Prediction Center Mesoanalysis indicated surface-based CAPE values as high as 2500 J/Kg at the onset of thunderstorm activity, while Precipitable Water values in the area were about 2.0 inches.  Nearby rain gauges indicated about 2.3 inches of rain fell in a 60-minute period, which is roughly a 10-year event per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Estimates.  Rainfall rates at times were recorded reaching in excess of 4 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rain and poor drainage resulted in flash flooding in the community of Appomattox, especially along Confederate Avenue where runoff became over 3 feet deep, with one witness observing water rising up to the hood of a stranded vehicle.  Nearby automated personal weather stations indicated about 1.5 inches of rain fell within a 30 to 40 minute period, with rainfall rates as high as 4 inches per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the midwestern United States brought a warm front across the region February 28th. Warm advection precipitation, enhanced by upslope flow and favorable upper jet dynamics, lead to an inch or two of rainfall in western portions of our forecast area. This, along with partial melting of snowpack in the mountains, lead to some isolated flooding across eastern western portions of our forecast area that continued into March 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mill Gap Rd. closed near the intersection with Lower Back Creek Rd. due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved out of the central Plains into the Ohio Valley, opening the Mid-Atlantic to southwesterly flow that was enriched with moisture. Along with the increasing moisture was rising temperatures, meaning precipitation types changed through the event as temperatures climbed. In the end, parts of Virginia received a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the county ranged from 2.0 inches of snowfall in Amherst to 6.0 inches four miles west of Pleasant View. Key impacts: slippery roads and sidewalks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the Ohio Valley. Warmer air aloft overran colder air in place that was associated with high pressure over New England, resulting in freezing rain. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved out of the central Plains into the Ohio Valley, opening the Mid-Atlantic to southwesterly flow that was enriched with moisture. Along with the increasing moisture was rising temperatures, meaning precipitation types changed through the event as temperatures climbed. In the end, parts of Virginia received a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the county ranged from 5.3 inches of snowfall three miles southwest of Evergreen to 6.0 inches in Appomattox. Key impacts: slippery roads and sidewalks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved out of the central Plains into the Ohio Valley, opening the Mid-Atlantic to southwesterly flow that was enriched with moisture. Along with the increasing moisture was rising temperatures, meaning precipitation types changed through the event as temperatures climbed. In the end, parts of Virginia received a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation amounts across the county ranged from 3.5 inches of snowfall in Mouth of Wilson to 5.0 inches of snowfall three miles south southwest of Elk Creek with 0.1 inches of sleet accumulation also measured three miles south southwest of Elk Creek. Key Impacts: slippery roads and sidewalks.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation amounts across the county ranged from 4.2 inches of snowfall one mile north northwest of Wythville to 5.0 inches of snowfall two miles north northeast of Coleman Store with 0.5 inches of sleet accumulation measured one mile north northwest of Wythville and 0.01 inches of ice accumulation from freezing rain two miles north of Patterson. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved out of the central Plains into the Ohio Valley, opening the Mid-Atlantic to southwesterly flow that was enriched with moisture. Along with the increasing moisture was rising temperatures, meaning precipitation types changed through the event as temperatures climbed. In the end, parts of Virginia received a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation amounts across the county ranged from 2.0 inches of snowfall one mile northwest of Chatham to 4.5 inches of snowfall three miles east northeast of Sago with 0.1 inches of sleet accumulation measured two miles east of Hurt and ice accumulation amounts from freezing rain of 0.25 inches measured three miles north northwest of Ringgold to 0.8 inches measured in Brosville. Key Impacts: slippery roads and sidewalks.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation amounts across the county ranged from 3.0 inches of snowfall in Snow Creek to 6.6 inches of snowfall one mile north northwest of Boones Mill with 0.1 inches of sleet accumulation as well as a trace of ice accumulation from freezing rain also measured in Snow Creek. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Closed low pressure slowly tracked into the Ohio Valley on the 31st before transferring its energy to low pressure off the Mid-Atlantic Coast on the 1st of February. The coastal low slowly tracked northeast while intensifying through midday on February the 2nd. ||Snow overspread the area on the 31st of January ahead of the first low. Drier air and warmer air moved in aloft, causing snow to change to a light wintry mix on the night of the 31st into the 1st. However, another period of snow developed as the coastal low strengthened to the east later on the 1st and that continued into the 2nd. ||Total snowfall accumulations ranged from 3 to 5 inches around Interstate 95, to around 4 to 8 inches over central Virginia into the central Shenandoah Valley, to around 8 to 16 inches across the northern Shenandoah Valley. Ice accumulation was around a tenth of an inch east of the Blue Ridge Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wintry precipitation continued at times into February 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system passed across the lower mid-Atlantic during the early morning hours of January 26th, which produced 1 to 2 inches of rain across portions of the mountains of Virginia.  Multiple rivers rose into Action Stage because of this rainfall, with a few rising above minor flood stage.  The rain also triggered at least one known debris flow.  Temperatures through much of the winter leading up to the event had often risen above freezing during the day and fallen below freezing at night, which could have enhanced the freeze-thaw cycle to weaken the integrity of rock formations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car wash sustained severe structural damage when the hillside immediately behind the building gave way and smashed through the rear wall of the building.

The coastal low moved out to sea later on the 19th. ||Canadian high pressure supplied plenty of low-level cold air, but warmer air worked its way in aloft, and this caused a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain. Snow and sleet overspread the area on the early morning of the 18th. Snow and sleet changed to a light wintry mix including freezing rain by the evening of the 18th. Precipitation ended as a period of light snow and sleet on the morning of the 19th. ||The highest snowfall accumulations (around 4 to 8 inches) were across the northern Shenandoah Valley toward the northern Blue Ridge Mountains of Virginia where the heaviest precipitation occurred, and temperatures aloft were cold enough for precipitation to start off as snow. However, mostly sleet occurred across central Virginia toward the Washington Metropolitan area, so accumulations were less (around an inch or two). ||Ice accumulation from freezing rain was up to a tenth of an inch for most areas, but around a tenth to a quarter inch in the Virginia Piedmont where warmer air aloft cause more freezing rain vs. snow or sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: VA-621 Bull Run Post Office Road was closed due to ice from freezing rain and an accident between Hollyspring Lane and Sudley Road around 9 PM on the evening of the 18th.||There were also multiple accidents from icy roads on VA-643 between VA-659 and VA-653.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in the southern stream of the jet passed by just to the south on the morning of the 7th. However, temperatures were marginal due to the lack of cold high pressure to the north. Therefore, precipitation began as rain east of the Blue Ridge Mountains where low-level warm air held on. However, colder air did eventually work its way in causing precipitation to change to snow. Farther west for locations near the Blue Ridge Mountains and westward, precipitation fell mainly in the form of snow. ||Snowfall accumulations ranged from around 1 to 2 inches near Interstate 95, with 3 to 6 inches near the Blue Ridge Mountains into the Shenandoah Valley. Snowfall accumulations were around 1 to 3 inches farther west toward the Potomac Highlands due to less precipitation.

Low pressure tracked into the Ohio Valley on the 13th before transferring its energy to a coastal low that moved offshore on the 14th. A period of sleet and freezing rain occurred across the area. Ice accumulations ranged from less than a tenth of an inch across the northern Shenandoah Valley, to 0.1-0.2 west of Interstate 95 into the central Shenandoah Valley, to around a quarter near and east of Interstate 95.

Low pressure tracked through the Great Lakes and the cold front associated with the low moved toward the area on the 22nd. A southerly flow ahead of the low and its associated cold front ushered in moisture from the Atlantic. Despite the southerly flow, there was still plenty of cold and dry air in place ahead of the system. Therefore, precipitation started off as snow and ice before eventually changing to rain. The heaviest snow was across the northern Shenandoah Valley with 1 to 2 inches for most areas. Elsewhere, snowfall amounts were generally less than one inch. ||Ice accumulation from freezing rain was less than a tenth of an inch for most areas and confined mainly to elevated surfaces near and west of Interstate 95.

A high impact event affected the Central Appalachians beginning on the morning of February 10th, as a wave of precipitation pressed into the area in response to a stationary boundary in close proximity. This resulted in a mixed bag of winter precipitation across the area, with significant ice and snowfall accumulations. Snow remained primarily north of southwest Virginia, but freezing rain resulted in a few hundredths of ice accumulation in Dickenson County.

Low pressure in the southern stream of the jet passed by just to the south on the morning of the 7th. However, temperatures were marginal due to the lack of cold high pressure to the north. Therefore, precipitation began as rain east of the Blue Ridge Mountains where low-level warm air held on. However, colder air did eventually work its way in causing precipitation to change to snow. Farther west for locations near the Blue Ridge Mountains and westward, precipitation fell mainly in the form of snow. ||Snowfall accumulations ranged from around 1 to 2 inches near Interstate 95, with 3 to 6 inches near the Blue Ridge Mountains into the Shenandoah Valley. Snowfall accumulations were around 1 to 3 inches farther west toward the Potomac Highlands due to less precipitation.

Low pressure tracked into the Ohio Valley on the 13th before transferring its energy to a coastal low that moved offshore on the 14th. A period of sleet and freezing rain occurred across the area. Ice accumulations ranged from less than a tenth of an inch across the northern Shenandoah Valley, to 0.1-0.2 west of Interstate 95 into the central Shenandoah Valley, to around a quarter near and east of Interstate 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex moved east from the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys across southwest Virginia. The system caused dozens of trees to be toppled across the region.  Heavy rainfall from these storms also caused localized flooding in the City of Roanoke, where rainfall rates were in excess of 4 inches per hour at one point, between a 5-year and 10-year rainfall event per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Frequency Estimates.  Rainfall amounts across the City of Roanoke ranged from 1.50 to 1.75 inches, not unusual for thunderstorm activity, but still enough to cause localized flooding given the more urbanized nature of the impacted area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew three trees down in, and near, Iron Gate. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex moved east from the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys across southwest Virginia. The system caused dozens of trees to be toppled across the region.  Heavy rainfall from these storms also caused localized flooding in the City of Roanoke, where rainfall rates were in excess of 4 inches per hour at one point, between a 5-year and 10-year rainfall event per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Frequency Estimates.  Rainfall amounts across the City of Roanoke ranged from 1.50 to 1.75 inches, not unusual for thunderstorm activity, but still enough to cause localized flooding given the more urbanized nature of the impacted area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew trees down along Fields Ave in Blue near Blue Ridge. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms associated with a warm front produced a tornado across portions of the Virginia Northern Neck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down along Richmond Road (Route 360) just southwest of Callao. The tornado was EF0 intensity at its beginning as it tracked northeast crossing Lakeside Drive just east of Callao. The tornado then crossed Northumberland Highway (Route 360), intensifying as it approached Chambers Lane. The tornado quickly reached EF2 intensity as it crossed Chambers Lane destroying one home and damaging several others. The destroyed home was built in the 1970s and based on builder inspector data was constructed to withstand 100 mph wind. In this same area, another home lost its garage causing severe damage to the home. In this area, winds were estimated around 120 mph at the strongest point of the tornado's path. The tornado continued northeast crossing Lewisetta Road and Old Farm Lane. Here, the tornado weakened to EF1 but continued to damage homes and outbuildings. The tornado continued moving northeast for another three quarters of a mile before lifting in the Coan River. Several eye witnesses reported the tornado lifting once it moved into the Coan River before the storm reached Lewisetta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple days of rainfall, which was heavy at times, produced saturated soils across much of Tazewell County, especially across the headwaters of the Clinch River.  During the early morning hours of March 1st, the cold front moved southward from the Ohio River Valley, bringing the axis of heavy rain across the county.  Clinch River basin average rainfall upstream of Richlands was 1.25 to 1.5 inches for the 24-hour period ending 8 am on the 1st, and 2.5 to 3 inches for the 72-hour period ending at 8 am on the 1st.  These amounts were sufficient to push the Clinch River at Richlands nearly a foot above minor flood stage, causing some flooding to roads near the river and surrounding some buildings with water during the morning and afternoon.  The river receded by early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cooperative Weather Observer at Richlands (RLCV2) recorded a 24-hour rainfall amount of 1.38 inches at 7 am on the 1st.  The 72-hour rainfall amount ending at 7 am on the 1st was 2.65 inches, with a storm-total amount of 2.75 inches as an additional 0.10 inches fell during late morning on the 1st before rain finally exited the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple days of rainfall, which was heavy at times, produced saturated soils across much of Tazewell County, especially across the headwaters of the Clinch River.  During the early morning hours of March 1st, the cold front moved southward from the Ohio River Valley, bringing the axis of heavy rain across the county.  Clinch River basin average rainfall upstream of Richlands was 1.25 to 1.5 inches for the 24-hour period ending 8 am on the 1st, and 2.5 to 3 inches for the 72-hour period ending at 8 am on the 1st.  These amounts were sufficient to push the Clinch River at Richlands nearly a foot above minor flood stage, causing some flooding to roads near the river and surrounding some buildings with water during the morning and afternoon.  The river receded by early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prolonged heavy rain atop saturated soils caused The Clinch River at Richlands to rise above the minor flood stage of 10 feet to crest at 10.91 feet at 12:45 pm on March 1st.  Basin average rainfall upstream of Richlands was 1.25 to 1.5 inches for the 24-hour period ending 8 am on the 1st, and 2.5 to 3 inches for the 72-hour period ending at 8 am on the 1st.  The river rose out of its banks into nearby Forth Street, impeding travel along the road.  The water rose into adjacent yards and threatened a few homes, but didn't rise high enough to enter the homes.  The Police Department was also surrounded by water, but water did not enter the building.  Flood waters receded during the afternoon as rain exited the region.  No damage was reported.

Clinch River basin average rainfall upstream of Richlands was 1.25 to 1.5 inches for the 24-hour period ending 8 am on the 1st, and 2.5 to 3 inches for the 72-hour period ending at 8 am on the 1st. The river receded by early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet weather station (KVATAZEW7) recorded 1.45 inches of rainfall during the 24-hour period ending 8 am on March 1st. The 72-hour total ending 8 am on the 1st was close to 3 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex moved east from the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys across southwest Virginia. The system caused dozens of trees to be toppled across the region.  Heavy rainfall from these storms also caused localized flooding in the City of Roanoke, where rainfall rates were in excess of 4 inches per hour at one point, between a 5-year and 10-year rainfall event per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Frequency Estimates.  Rainfall amounts across the City of Roanoke ranged from 1.50 to 1.75 inches, not unusual for thunderstorm activity, but still enough to cause localized flooding given the more urbanized nature of the impacted area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a large tree down on a residence along Hemlock Drive. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of June 9th in an environment of strong afternoon heating as temperatures warmed into the low 80s. CAPE values ranged from 1,500 to 2,000 J/Kg and Precipitable Water values were observed to be in the 1.6 to 1.7 inch range. The combination of instability and abundant moisture resulted in storms producing localized rainfall rates as high as 4 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Little Rock Creek was observed flowing out of its banks, with water a few inches deep across Sugar Shack Road.  The road was closed as a precaution for increased flooding.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure sitting over the upper Mississippi Valley and high pressure situated off the coast of the Atlantic placed the region into a persistent southwesterly flow, allowing for a very saturated air-mass that worked with diurnal heating to produce a suitable environment for pulse severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced damaging winds gusts resulting in downed power lines in the town of Ironto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of June 11th in an environment of strong afternoon heating as temperatures warmed into the low to mid 80s ahead of a backdoor cold front draped across northern Virginia. CAPE values were in excess of 2,000 J/Kg and Precipitable Water values were observed to be in the 1.8 to 1.9 inch range. Weak atmospheric winds resulted in slow storm motion.  The combination of deep instability and abundant moisture resulted in storms producing high rainfall rates of 2 to locally 5 inches per hour, with 1-hour rainfall totals exceeding 3.2 inches near the City of Lynchburg.  This rainfall is a 100-year event per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates.  One swift water rescue was reported in the City of Lynchburg when a vehicle was stranded in the floodwaters from Blackwater Creek.  No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to locally in excess of four inches of rain caused a mudslide along Highway 501.  The road was passable after the debris was cleared.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms trained across southern Highland County, forming in an environment of CAPE exceeding 2,500 J/Kg and Precipitable Water of around 1.5 inches.  The storms produced 2.5 to 4 inches of rainfall amounts over a 2 to 3-hour period, with rainfall rates at times reaching 5 to 6 inches per hour.  Steep terrain in the Back Creek basin aided in creating efficient runoff to cause the stream gauge at Back Creek at Sunrise to rise to 10.01 feet, exceeding Moderate Flood Stage set at 10 feet.  This is the highest this gauge has risen since 2008.  FLASH estimated that rainfall just upstream from the stream gauge was over 3 times greater than 1-hour Flash Flood Guidance.  CREST Unit Streamflow was estimating over 1,000 cfs per square mile, whiile CREST 1-hour Average Return Interval estimates put this rainfall at well over a 200-year event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly 3 inches of rain fell from a thunderstorm inside of a 1 hour period of time in the Big Island area, including along Camden Road.  Rainfall rates were observed to be as high as 5 inches per hour at times during the event.  Runoff from this rainfall caused Camden Road to become flooded by several inches of water and impassible.  Water was also reported by law enforcement entering a basement of a home.  FLASH (Flooded Locations and Simulated Hydrographs Project) estimated that 1-hour rainfall was about 2.5 times that of Flash Flood guidance and that this was a 25 to 50-year event per FLASH 1-Hour ARI estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of June 11th in an environment of strong afternoon heating as temperatures warmed into the low to mid 80s ahead of a backdoor cold front draped across northern Virginia. CAPE values were in excess of 2,000 J/Kg and Precipitable Water values were observed to be in the 1.8 to 1.9 inch range. Weak atmospheric winds resulted in slow storm motion.  The combination of deep instability and abundant moisture resulted in storms producing high rainfall rates of 2 to locally 5 inches per hour, with 1-hour rainfall totals exceeding 3.2 inches near the City of Lynchburg.  This rainfall is a 100-year event per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates.  One swift water rescue was reported in the City of Lynchburg when a vehicle was stranded in the floodwaters from Blackwater Creek.  No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 2 and 2.5 inches of rain fell within a 90-minute period across the community of Boonsboro, at times falling at rates of 4 to 5 inches per hour. This is roughly a 10-year rainfall event per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates. This rainfall caused flooding across Walnut Hollow Road at Indered Farm Road.  The road was reopened and passable after the water receded.  No damage was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 2.5 and 3.5 inches of rain fell from a slow-moving thunderstorm across the Blackwater Creek watershed during a 90-minute period.  Rainfall rates were observed to 2 to 3 inches per hour during much of this event.  This caused flooding along Blackwater Creek, which caused water to enter apartments along Sandusky Drive.  Multiple residents of these apartments were forced to evacuate until the water receded.  In addition, an automobile became stranded in the flood water along Greenwood Drive where it crosses Blackwater Creek.  No injuries were reported and the occupants were able to escape the flooded vehicle and move to a safe location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure sitting over the upper Mississippi Valley and high pressure situated off the coast of the Atlantic placed the region into a persistent southwesterly flow, allowing for a very saturated air-mass that worked with diurnal heating to produce a suitable environment for pulse severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm producing damaging wind gusts blew several tops of trees down along Summer Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms in advance of low pressure and an associated frontal boundary produced damaging winds, large hail, and one tornado across portions of southeast Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down just east of Route 258 near the Town of Walters. The damage began as it crossed Stevens Drive, where large tree branches were broken. The tornado then tracked east northeast and uprooted numerous trees near Ballard Road. After crossing Ballard Road, the tornado knocked down a pole barn, destroyed a garage, and damaged a roof of a house near Collosse Road. The tornado continued to track eastward, roughly following Rhodes Drive, where it blew a roof off of a church near Little Norfolk Road. The tornado did more tree damage before lifting shortly after crossing Spivey Town Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of Tropical Storm Elsa tracked north just inland of the Middle Atlantic Coast from Thursday morning through Thursday night, July 8th. The Tropical Storm produced tropical storm force winds and associated wind damage across portions of eastern and southeast Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely low precipitation amounts during November 2021 across the southern Piedmont of Virginia resulted in the onset of Severe Drought conditions (D2 per the U.S. Drought Monitor classifications for drought severity).  Less than 5 percent of the normal monthly precipitation for November fell across portions of Halifax, Charlotte and Pittsylvania Counties, greatly intensifying dryness that was already in place during September and October.  As a result of the dryness, streamflows across the region were below-normal to well below-normal for the end of November and soil moisture had diminished to levels of severe to locally extreme dryness.  Because of the late time of the autumn and the onset of cooler temperatures, agriculture was not as highly impacted as it would have been during the warm season, and some farmers welcomed the dryness as it allowed them to work in their fields without contending with mud.  Concerns for increased wildfire activity were present, with county officials across the region either having already implemented burn bans by the end of the month, or were in the planning stages of implementing burn bans.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought conditions were declared for the extreme southeastern portion of Pittsylvania County on November 30th after a month of extremely low rainfall totals. The ASOS At Danville Regional Airport (DAN) measured only 0.15 inches of precipitation for the month, or only about 4 percent of the normal amount of 3.46 inches.  This makes November 2021 both the driest November and the 4th-driest month on record dating as far back as 1916.  Because of the dry conditions, officials for both the City of Danville and Pittsylvania County issued a burn ban effective 6:00 pm on the 30th preventing any type of outdoor burning. NASA SPoRT LIS data estimated that average soil moisture in the top 100cm soil was around the second percentile, an indicator of extreme dryness, and county streamflows ranged from below-normal to well below-normal per the USGS WaterWatch website.  However, impacts to agriculture and livestock were limited given the late time of the year.  No wildland fires were reported burning that day within the county or the City of Danville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly flow allowed for plenty of moisture ahead of a cold front. The moisture along with an unstable atmosphere caused heavy rain to be associated with thunderstorms, and this led to instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The water level rose above flood stage along the Accotink Creek at Strathmeade Springs. The water level crested at 252.76 feet on July 1st at 11:00 PM EST, before falling below flood stage at midnight. Woodburn Road and Prosperity Avenue were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms produced two tornadoes, hail, and widespread wind damage (some significant) across the DC metro and central Maryland, with spottier wind damage extending into the Baltimore area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down at 8:59 PM EDT in the Waverly Hills community of Arlington. Numerous trees were snapped in multiple directions in this community, notably in and around Woodstock Park and in the backyards of residences in the 4500 block of 20th Place North in Arlington. One tree in Woodstock Park had its bark removed as a result of the wind. As the tornado crossed into the Cherrydale neighborhood of Arlington, several trees were uprooted and numerous large branches were snapped, some blocking roads and downing power lines. Some homes had siding and shingles removed, and a few windows were broken from falling trees. Several homes also had porch railing and gutter damage with doors blown open.||The tornado continued east over the Lyon Village neighborhood, downing several trees and numerous branches across the north end of the community, including around the community center. The tornado weakened slightly as it moved into the Courthouse community, passing just north of Key Elementary School, continuing to snap large branches and down power lines. Damage became hard to identify as the circulation crossed the highly urbanized area of Rosslyn, but emerged again after the tornado crossed the Potomac River and entered the District of Columbia at 9:03 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely low precipitation amounts during November 2021 across the southern Piedmont of Virginia resulted in the onset of Severe Drought conditions (D2 per the U.S. Drought Monitor classifications for drought severity).  Less than 5 percent of the normal monthly precipitation for November fell across portions of Halifax, Charlotte and Pittsylvania Counties, greatly intensifying dryness that was already in place during September and October.  As a result of the dryness, streamflows across the region were below-normal to well below-normal for the end of November and soil moisture had diminished to levels of severe to locally extreme dryness.  Because of the late time of the autumn and the onset of cooler temperatures, agriculture was not as highly impacted as it would have been during the warm season, and some farmers welcomed the dryness as it allowed them to work in their fields without contending with mud.  Concerns for increased wildfire activity were present, with county officials across the region either having already implemented burn bans by the end of the month, or were in the planning stages of implementing burn bans.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought conditions were declared for the southern half of Charlotte County on November 30th after a month of extremely low rainfall totals. The CoCoRaHS observer at Saxe 4.3 SE (VA-CH-1) measured only 0.10 inches of precipitation for the month, less than 3 percent of the normal amount. No burn bans were put into effect during the month however. NASA SPoRT LIS data estimated that average soil moisture in the top 100cm soil was between the 10th and 5th percentiles, an indicator of severe dryness, and county streamflows below-normal per the USGS WaterWatch website. There were no reported impacts to agriculture and livestock given the late time of the year. There were no wildland fires were reported burning within the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As Tropical Storm Elsa moved through southern Virginia, a band of showers and thunderstorms pushed across Suffolk. Within that band of storms, a brief tornado touched down southwest of Suffolk. Trees were damaged from near Lummis Road and Quaker Road up to Quince Road. A few trees were snapped and limbs torn out of tree tops.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall total of 2.58 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa passed northward across eastern Virginia during the afternoon and evening of July 8th, 2021.  While the vast majority of heavier rainfall fell to the east of the NWS Blacksburg forecast area, a portion of the deformation zone developed over Halifax and Charlotte Counties, resulting in as much as 3.5 inches of rain during a 6-hour period, with the heaviest amounts along the eastern border of these counties.  At the time, these counties were experiencing moderate drought conditions per the United States Drought Monitor, with 90-day precipitation leading up to the event some 3 to 6 inches below normal. As such, this rainfall was seen as beneficial to the area.  Rainfall rates seldom exceeded 1 inch per hour, and no flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal automated weather station (Weather Underground: KVAHALIF9) recorded 1.92 inches of rainfall during a 6-hour period with the passage of Tropical Storm Elsa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and prefrontal trough tracking from the Ohio and Tennessee River Valleys dove southeast into our region Saturday afternoon. These two features triggered numerous showers and storms that feed off of CAPE values greater than 1,500 j/kg and  PWATs that approach 1.5 to 1.7 inches. The storm mode started as multicellular clusters then transitioned into organized line segments as deep layer shear strengthened. This promoted areas of wind damage along with instances of large hail and localized flash flooding during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was struck by lightning in the community of Laurel Fork, VA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front combined with shortwave energy pushed through the region during the afternoon of July 26th, 2021. Widespread showers and storms develop as a result of an unstable airmass supported by CAPE values of 2,000-4000 j/kg. This contributed to the storms producing damaging winds through much of the afternoon and into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blow off a portion of a tin roof on Thrasher Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled along the Virginia and North Carolina border combined with heat and moisture triggered another round of thunderstorms. Some of these storms produced damaging winds and large hail, especially along and east of the Blue Ridge mountains.   Single cell to multicell clusters develop between Lexington, VA and Galax, VA late in the afternoon and evening before slowly shifting south and east overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A half dozen trees were blown down just off of Big Island Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening of July 11th.  A few of the storms proved capable of producing sporadic damaging winds and locally heavy rainfall, supported by strong instability with CAPE values reaching as high as 2,000 J/Kg across the mountains by late afternoon.  Precipitable water values at 1.5 inches, was an indication of a moist enough atmosphere to support heavy rain.  The stronger storms were observed producing rainfall amounts as high as 5 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated two inches of rain fell within an hour from a slow-moving thunderstorm across the Dry Creek drainage basin, between a 10- and 25-year rainfall event per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates.  The rain was estimated to be falling at rates of three to four inches per hour per KFCX Doppler radar, and caused Dry Creek to overflow its banks and flow across Camp Road several inches deep.  No damage to the road was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree was downed across Louisa Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite prolonged periods of dry weather before the event, deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico pooled across the lower Mid-Atlantic ahead of an approaching cold front.  Precipitable water values by the afternoon of July 1st were estimated to have reached the 1.8 to 2 inch range.  Strong afternoon heating, combined with steepened lapse rates associated with a passing upper level disturbance, produced a highly unstable atmosphere  causing localized wind damage from severe thunderstorms. While widespread rainfall was beneficial for most, thunderstorms began to train over a few locations, producing prolonged rainfall rates of 3 to as high as 5 inches per hour, resulting in flash flooding for some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CoCoRaHS observer at Blacksburg 1.7 ESE recorded a 24-hour rainfall total of 4.37 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated weather station on the campus of Virginia Tech University (KVABLACK72) observed 2.54 inches of rainfall inside of a 60 minute period, caused by training thunderstorm activity. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep moist southwest flow prevailed over the region on July 13th with a bulging Bermuda High to the east and an upper level trough digging in from the Great Lakes region. Air temps in the mid to upper 80s and low 90s created an unstable environment to trigger a few severe thunderstorms that produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down two trees and several small limbs in the Mt. Tabor Village area. A tree was also blown down at the intersection of Mt. Tabor Road and Bishop Road. Power was reported out across the neighborhood as a result. Pea size hail was also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving series of localized thunderstorms produced intense rainfall in excess of 11 inches during a 3- to 4-hour period of time during the evening of July 24th, 2021, which is an exceedingly rare event.  NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Estimates put this amount of rain for a 6-hour duration at well over an 1000-year rainfall event, or less than a 0.1% of occurring during any given year. Nearly all of the 10.1 miles^2 (per USGS Stream Stats) of the Sycamore Creek basin received heavy rain.  A National Water Model simulation of streamflow at the mouth of Sycamore Creek where it empties into the James River calculated a flow of 3,979 cfs.  If close to accurate, this flow would be between a 50- and 100-year flooding event per USGS Stream Stats.  However, as the basin is heavily wooded and sparsely populated, and given that the rain fell on a Saturday evening when commuting would be at a minimum, very few impacts were observed and/or reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from several inches of rain caused Sycamore Creek to rise out of its banks and flow across Sycamore Creek Road near the intersection of Woodlawn Church Road.  At around 9:40 pm EST, the rainfall triggered at least one debris flow nearby.  The flooding left large amounts of debris along the bridge crossing the creek and across the roadway.  Structural damage to the road was also observed and required repair.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Elsa passed northward across eastern Virginia during the afternoon and evening of July 8th, 2021.  While the vast majority of heavier rainfall fell to the east of the NWS Blacksburg forecast area, a portion of the deformation zone developed over Halifax and Charlotte Counties, resulting in as much as 3.5 inches of rain during a 6-hour period, with the heaviest amounts along the eastern border of these counties.  At the time, these counties were experiencing moderate drought conditions per the United States Drought Monitor, with 90-day precipitation leading up to the event some 3 to 6 inches below normal. As such, this rainfall was seen as beneficial to the area.  Rainfall rates seldom exceeded 1 inch per hour, and no flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal automated weather station (Weather Underground: KVANATHA3) recorded 1.82 inches of rainfall during a 6-hour period with the passage of Tropical Storm Elsa, which is between a 2- and a 5-year rainfall event per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite prolonged periods of dry weather before the event, deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico pooled across the lower Mid-Atlantic ahead of an approaching cold front.  Precipitable water values by the afternoon of July 1st were estimated to have reached the 1.8 to 2 inch range.  Strong afternoon heating, combined with steepened lapse rates associated with a passing upper level disturbance, produced a highly unstable atmosphere  causing localized wind damage from severe thunderstorms. While widespread rainfall was beneficial for most, thunderstorms began to train over a few locations, producing prolonged rainfall rates of 3 to as high as 5 inches per hour, resulting in flash flooding for some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew two trees down on Morehead Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled along the Virginia and North Carolina border combined with heat and moisture triggered another round of thunderstorms. Some of these storms produced damaging winds and large hail, especially along and east of the Blue Ridge mountains.   Single cell to multicell clusters develop between Lexington, VA and Galax, VA late in the afternoon and evening before slowly shifting south and east overnight.
A ridge of high pressure and southwesterly flow allowed heat and humidity to build across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices rose to between 105 and 110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and prefrontal trough tracking from the Ohio and Tennessee River Valleys dove southeast into our region Saturday afternoon. These two features triggered numerous showers and storms that feed off of CAPE values greater than 1,500 j/kg and  PWATs that approach 1.5 to 1.7 inches. The storm mode started as multicellular clusters then transitioned into organized line segments as deep layer shear strengthened. This promoted areas of wind damage along with instances of large hail and localized flash flooding during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees blown down by thunderstorm winds at the intersection of While Rock Road and West Midland Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front dropping down from the north along with several pieces of shortwave energy triggered multiple rounds of showers and storms. Modest instability and shear supported single cell and multicell storm clusters during the afternoon and evening hours. CAPE values greater than 2,000 j/kg could be found over the region creating an unstable atmosphere. Steepening lapse rates aided by good differential heating and pooling of moisture ahead of the encroaching front enhanced the severe threats of localized damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down along Lexington Turnpike due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely low precipitation amounts during November 2021 across the southern Piedmont of Virginia resulted in the onset of Severe Drought conditions (D2 per the U.S. Drought Monitor classifications for drought severity).  Less than 5 percent of the normal monthly precipitation for November fell across portions of Halifax, Charlotte and Pittsylvania Counties, greatly intensifying dryness that was already in place during September and October.  As a result of the dryness, streamflows across the region were below-normal to well below-normal for the end of November and soil moisture had diminished to levels of severe to locally extreme dryness.  Because of the late time of the autumn and the onset of cooler temperatures, agriculture was not as highly impacted as it would have been during the warm season, and some farmers welcomed the dryness as it allowed them to work in their fields without contending with mud.  Concerns for increased wildfire activity were present, with county officials across the region either having already implemented burn bans by the end of the month, or were in the planning stages of implementing burn bans.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought conditions were declared for the southern half of Halifax County on November 30th after a month of extremely low rainfall totals. The Cooperative Weather Observer (COOP) at South Boston (SOBV2) measured only 0.33 inches of precipitation for the month, nearly 10 percent of the normal amount of 3.40 inches. This month was the 2nd driest November on record for this COOP dating back to 1971, and the 4th driest month overall. No burn bans were put into effect in the county during the month. NASA SPoRT LIS data estimated that average soil moisture in the top 100cm soil ranged between the 10th and 2nd percentiles, an indicator of severe to locally extreme dryness, and county streamflows varied between below-normal and well below-normal per the USGS WaterWatch website. There were no reported impacts to agriculture and livestock given the late time of the year. There were no wildland fires were reported burning within the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally low precipitation totals during December 2021, combined with unusually warm average temperatures (6F-8F degree above normal) through the month, allowed Severe Drought conditions (D2 per the U.S. Drought Monitor classifications for drought severity) to persist across the southern Piedmont of Virginia since its onset in late November.  While not as dry as November, less than half of the normal monthly precipitation for December fell across portions of Halifax, Charlotte and Pittsylvania Counties, cementing the dryness that was already in place during much of autumn.  Streamflows and soil moisture continued to range from below-normal to well below-normal for early winter. Agricultural concerns were minimal due to winter dormancy, however slow/poor growth of cover crops, which are meant to reduce soil erosion, were noted.  Conversely, some farmers welcomed the dryness as it allowed them to work in their fields without contending with mud.  Concerns for increased wildfire activity were present, burn bans already in place at the start of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought conditions declared for the southern half of Charlotte County at the end of November persisted through December after another month of precipitation that was significantly below normal. The CoCoRaHS observer at Saxe 4.3 SE (VA-CH-1) measured 1.17 inches of precipitation for the month, about 30 percent of the normal amount. NASA SPoRT LIS data estimated that average soil moisture in the top 100 cm soil at the start of the month was between the 10th and 5th percentiles, an indicator of severe dryness, and only decreased below the 2nd percentile by the end of the month. County streamflows remained below normal through the month per the USGS WaterWatch website. There were no reported impacts to agriculture and livestock given the late time of the year. A burn ban was put into effect for the county on December 2nd, and lifted December 20th after a widespread rainfall event. There were no wildland fires reported within the county.

Concerns for increased wildfire activity were present, burn bans already in place at the start of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought conditions declared for the extreme southeastern portion of Pittsylvania County at the end of November persisted through December after another month of precipitation that was significantly below normal. The ASOS at the Danville Regional Airport (DAN) measured only 0.65 inches of precipitation for the month, or only about 21 percent of the normal amount of 3.06 inches. NASA SPoRT LIS data estimated that average soil moisture in the top 100 cm soil at the start of the month was between the 10th and 5th percentiles, an indicator of severe dryness, and only decreased below the 2nd percentile by the end of the month. Local streamflows, including the Dan River at Danville (DVLV2), remained below normal through the month per the USGS WaterWatch website. There were few impacts to agriculture and livestock given the late time of the year, affecting cover crops designed to reduce soil erosion with only miniscule growth. It is not known if this resulted in a financial impact as cover crops are not harvested. A burn ban, implemented in late November, remained in effect for the county and the City of Danville for the entire month, however there were no wildland fires reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The water level along the Accotink Creek at Strathmeade Springs rapidly rose above flood stage at 6:46 AM EST, cresting at 254.54 feet at 9:35 AM EST. The water level fell below flood stage at 11:48 AM EST. Woodburn Road and Prosperity Avenue were flooded by at least six inches of flowing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed in an environment of strong daytime heating on August 19th, allowing for deep instability as surface-based CAPE values were observed to be in the 1,500 to 2,500 J/Kg range during the evening for areas mostly along and east of the Blue Ridge.  The thunderstorms were fueled by deep tropical moisture, with precipitable water observed to be 1.90 inches per the August 20/00Z upper air sounding conducted by the NWS in Blacksburg.  One thunderstorm developed over the city of Roanoke shortly after 8:00 pm EDT, followed by another that moved across the city around 9:00 pm EDT.  Doppler radar estimated that between 3 and 4 inches of rain fell across portions of the city, including the downtown area, during a 75- to 90-minute period.  Two automated rain gauges, located nearby the areas of radar-estimated greatest rainfall, recorded between 2 and 2.5 inches of rain within 75 minutes, both of which are between 10- and 25-year rainfall events per NOAA Atlas 14 Point Precipitation Frequency estimates.  Given the urbanized nature of where the rainfall occurred, much of the rainfall converted directly into runoff, overwhelming the city's storm drain system.  Multiple streets near the downtown area became flooded with 1 to 2 feet of mainly standing water, but water was an estimated 4 feet deep in a few spots.  Numerous cars parked along the flooded streets had water enter the passenger area.  At least 5 water rescues were conducted by the City of Roanoke Fire & Rescue as cars attempted to drive through the flood waters and stalled.  No injuries were reported.  About an hour after heavy rain ended, the flooding had abated and most of the previously-flooded roads were passable again.  Most of this rain fell across the Tinker Creek watershed, causing the stream gauge for Tinker Creek Above Glade Creek to register a 4.5 foot rise to crest just above Action Stage (7.0 feet), before water receded again.  The National Water Model estimated the peak flow at this stream gauge to be around 2,000 cfs, which is well under a 2 year event per USGS Stream Stats.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Salem Avenue and Campbell Avenue were flooded with up to 4 feet of standing water as heavy rain overwhelmed the storm sewer system.  Five water rescues were performed as cars drove into the flood waters, causing the vehicles to stall.  No injuries were reported.
